original_abstract:
While Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have achieved remarkable success in single-label image classification, their application to multi-label scenarios remains difficult due to complex spatial layouts, object occlusions, and a lack of large-scale annotated multi-label datasets. To address these challenges, we introduce a flexible deep learning architecture termed Hypotheses-CNN-Pooling (HCP). Our approach utilizes a set of object segment hypotheses as input, processes them through a shared CNN, and aggregates the results using a max-pooling operation to generate final multi-label predictions.

The HCP framework offers several key advantages: it eliminates the need for ground-truth bounding box annotations during training, significantly reducing the labeling burden; it is intrinsically robust to noisy or redundant hypotheses through its pooling mechanism; and it does not require explicit labels for individual hypotheses. Furthermore, the underlying shared CNN can be initialized using parameters pre-trained on large-scale single-label datasets like ImageNet, mitigating the issue of limited multi-label training data. Evaluated on the Pascal VOC2007 and VOC2012 benchmarks, HCP consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods. Notably, HCP achieves an mAP of 84.2% on VOC2012, which rises to 90.3% when fused with complementary hand-crafted features, surpassing previous best results by a margin of more than 7%.
humanized_abstract:
Despite the success of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) in many single-label image classification applications, extending them to multi-label image classification is non-trivial due to their inherent difficulties in dealing with complexities of spatial relationships among objects, object overlapping and the scarcity of large-scale multi-label annotated training datasets. This work proposes a flexible deep learning network known as Hypotheses-CNN-Pooling (HCP). The proposed network takes a set of object segment hypotheses as inputs, and after being passed through a shared CNN, the outputs are merged with max-pooling, resulting in a set of predicted multi-label outputs.

Our approach, known as HCP, has the following properties. - They eliminate the need for any ground-truth bounding box annotations during training. Hence, they eliminate a substantial portion of labeling required in current learning-based detection frameworks. - They are naturally robust to noise or over-explaining a scene due to the nature of the pooling operation. - They do not need labeling of individual detection hypotheses. - The shared deep neural network can leverage models pre-trained on the massive, yet commonly used, single-label ImageNet training set. Experiments on the Pascal VOC2007 and VOC2012 benchmarks confirm the advantage of HCP over the state of the art. While our results show an improvement in terms of mAP by 5 points over the best existing detection framework (by only using features from our classification branch) on the VOC2007 dataset and by 4 points (which translates into 84.2% vs. 80.2%) on the VOC2012 dataset, our method yields an mAP of 90.3% (which is at least 7+ points ahead of the state of the art) when we further enhance our results using joint prediction and complementary, carefully-designed hand-crafted features on VOC2012.

document.output:
Despite the success of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) in many single-label image classification applications, extending them to multi-label image classification is non-trivial due to their inherent difficulties in dealing with complexities of spatial relationships among objects, object overlapping and the scarcity of large-scale multi-label annotated training datasets. The proposed network takes a set of object segment hypotheses as inputs, and after being passed through a shared CNN, the outputs are merged with max-pooling, resulting in a set of predicted multi-label outputs.

Our approach, known as HCP, has the following properties. - They eliminate the need for any ground-truth bounding box annotations during training. Hence, they eliminate a substantial portion of labeling required in current learning-based detection frameworks. - They are naturally robust to noise or over-explaining a scene due to the nature of the pooling operation. - They do not need labeling of individual detection hypotheses. - The shared deep neural network can leverage models pre-trained on the massive, yet commonly used, single-label ImageNet training set. Experiments on the Pascal VOC2007 and VOC2012 benchmarks confirm the advantage of HCP over the state of the art. While our results show an improvement in terms of mAP by 5 points over the best existing detection framework (by only using features from our classification branch) on the VOC2007 dataset and by 4 points (which translates into 84.2% vs. 80.2%) on the VOC2012 dataset, our method yields an mAP of 90.3% (which is at least 7+ points ahead of the state of the art) when we further enhance our results using joint prediction and complementary, carefully-designed hand-crafted features on VOC2012.
document.input:
While Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have achieved remarkable success in single-label image classification, their application to multi-label scenarios remains difficult due to complex spatial layouts, object occlusions, and a lack of large-scale annotated multi-label datasets. To address these challenges, we introduce a flexible deep learning architecture termed Hypotheses-CNN-Pooling (HCP). Our approach utilizes a set of object segment hypotheses as input, processes them through a shared CNN, and aggregates the results using a max-pooling operation to generate final multi-label predictions.

The HCP framework offers several key advantages: it eliminates the need for ground-truth bounding box annotations during training, significantly reducing the labeling burden; it is intrinsically robust to noisy or redundant hypotheses through its pooling mechanism; and it does not require explicit labels for individual hypotheses. Furthermore, the underlying shared CNN can be initialized using parameters pre-trained on large-scale single-label datasets like ImageNet, mitigating the issue of limited multi-label training data. Evaluated on the Pascal VOC2007 and VOC2012 benchmarks, HCP consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods. Notably, HCP achieves an mAP of 84.2% on VOC2012, which rises to 90.3% when fused with complementary hand-crafted features, surpassing previous best results by a margin of more than 7%.
